summary:
The Gumelnița culture was a Chalcolithic culture of southern Romania and northern Bulgaria, flourishing between approximately 4600 and 3900 BCE. It represents one of the most developed prehistoric cultures of the lower Danube basin and is closely connected to the broader complex of Chalcolithic cultures in southeastern Europe that included the Kodjadermen and Karanovo VI cultures of Bulgaria. Gumelnița communities inhabited tell settlements — mounds formed by the accumulated debris of successive occupations — that sometimes rose to impressive heights above the surrounding plain, attesting to centuries of continuous habitation.

Gumelnița culture is characterized by rich material culture including fine painted pottery, an extensive tradition of clay anthropomorphic figurines, copper tools and ornaments, and evidence of sophisticated craft production. The culture participated in the same Chalcolithic metalworking tradition that produced the remarkable gold and copper artifacts of the [[event:1559|Varna Necropolis]], and Gumelnița sites have yielded copper axes, chisels, and ornaments in significant quantities. The flat tells of the lower Danube plain provided stable, defensible settlement locations and access to the rich agricultural soils of the Wallachian plain.

Gumelnița society shows evidence of social stratification and craft specialization, reflecting the growing complexity of Chalcolithic communities in southeastern Europe. The culture declined around 3900–3800 BCE, possibly due to pressure from [[event:1538|Pontic steppe]] pastoralists moving westward or from climatic deterioration affecting agricultural productivity.
